Optimal Matching between Point CloudsMonge Problem between MeasuresKantorovich RelaxationDual ProblemSemi-discrete and Wasserstein-1Divergences and Dual NormsEntropic Regularization: Sinkhorn AlgorithmEntropic Regularization: ConvergenceGeneralized Wasserstein DistancesGeneralized OT ProblemsBeyond Comparing MeasuresDynamic Optimal TransportWasserstein Gradient FlowsGenerative Models via Transportation

Optimal Matching between Point Clouds

This opening chapter isolates the simplest form of optimal transport: pairing two finite point clouds. The stakes are algorithmic and geometric at once: one sees the combinatorial nature of transport, the special simplicity of the line, and the first hints that convex relaxation will be necessary in higher dimension. Classical assignment algorithms such as the Hungarian method and auction methods Kuhn, 1955Bertsekas, 1992 provide the computational backdrop, while the geometric examples prepare the Kantorovich relaxation.

from pathlib import Path
import sys

from IPython.display import Image as DisplayImage
from IPython.display import display

here = Path.cwd()
myst_dir = None
for candidate in [here, here.parent, here / "myst", here.parent / "myst", here.parent.parent / "myst"]:
    if (candidate / "ot4ml_web.py").exists():
        myst_dir = candidate.resolve()
        sys.path.insert(0, str(myst_dir))
        break

if myst_dir is None:
    raise RuntimeError("Could not locate myst/ot4ml_web.py")

repo_root = myst_dir.parent
thumbnails = repo_root / "notebooks-figures" / "thumbnails"

def show_book_figure(name, width=760):
    display(DisplayImage(filename=str(thumbnails / f"{name}.png"), width=width))

# Static figures are Python-rendered; interactive demos are browser-rendered.
from ot4ml_web import (
    plot_histogram_equalization,
    plot_cost_power_sweep,
    plot_quantile_matching,
    plot_regularization_sweep,
)

Monge Problem for Discrete Points¶

This section formulates matching as Monge’s deterministic transport problem on two equally weighted clouds. The one-dimensional case is a transparent reference case where the optimal map can be read off by sorting.

Matching Problem¶

Given a cost matrix (Ci,j)i∈{1,…,n},j∈{1,…,m}(C_{i,j})_{i \in \{1,\ldots,n\}, j \in \{1,\ldots,m\}}(Ci,j​)i∈{1,…,n},j∈{1,…,m}​ and assuming n=mn=mn=m, the optimal assignment problem aims to find a bijection σ\sigmaσ within the set Perm(n)\Perm(n)Perm(n) of permutations of nnn elements that solves

min⁡σ∈Perm(n)1n∑i=1nCi,σ(i).\min_{\sigma \in \Perm(n)} \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n C_{i,\sigma(i)}.σ∈Perm(n)min​n1​i=1∑n​Ci,σ(i)​.
(1)

One could naively evaluate the cost above for all permutations in Perm(n)\Perm(n)Perm(n). However, this set has size n!n!n!, which becomes enormous even for small values of nnn. In general, the optimal σ\sigmaσ is not unique.

One-Dimensional Case¶

In one dimension, convex costs select monotone matchings.

Proposition: Monotone Matching on the Line

Assume that the points (xi)i(x_i)_i(xi​)i​ and (yj)j(y_j)_j(yj​)j​ are pairwise distinct. If the cost is of the form Ci,j=h(xi−yj)C_{i,j}=h(x_i-y_j)Ci,j​=h(xi​−yj​), where h:R→R+h:\RR\to\RR^+h:R→R+ is strictly convex, for instance Ci,j=∣xi−yj∣pC_{i,j}=|x_i-y_j|^pCi,j​=∣xi​−yj​∣p with p>1p>1p>1, then any optimal σ\sigmaσ defines a strictly increasing map xi↦yσ(i)x_i \mapsto y_{\sigma(i)}xi​↦yσ(i)​, and hence is unique:

∀(i,i′),(xi−xi′)(yσ(i)−yσ(i′))>0.\forall (i,i'), \qquad (x_i-x_{i'})(y_{\sigma(i)}-y_{\sigma(i')}) > 0.∀(i,i′),(xi​−xi′​)(yσ(i)​−yσ(i′)​)>0.
(2)
Proof

If this property is violated, there exists (i,i′)(i,i')(i,i′) such that (xi−xi′)(yσ(i)−yσ(i′))<0(x_i-x_{i'})(y_{\sigma(i)}-y_{\sigma(i')}) < 0(xi​−xi′​)(yσ(i)​−yσ(i′)​)<0. One can then define a permutation σ~\tilde\sigmaσ~ by swapping the match: σ~(i)=σ(i′)\tilde\sigma(i)=\sigma(i')σ~(i)=σ(i′) and σ~(i′)=σ(i)\tilde\sigma(i')=\sigma(i)σ~(i′)=σ(i). This gives a strictly better cost by the following elementary inequality.

Let h:R→Rh:\RR\to\RRh:R→R be strictly convex and let x<x′x<x'x<x′ and y<y′y<y'y<y′. Then

h(x−y)+h(x′−y′)<h(x−y′)+h(x′−y).h(x-y)+h(x'-y') < h(x-y')+h(x'-y).h(x−y)+h(x′−y′)<h(x−y′)+h(x′−y).
(3)

Set d:=y′−y>0d:=y'-y>0d:=y′−y>0 and define, for every s∈Rs\in\RRs∈R,

D(s):=h(s)−h(s−d)dD(s):=\frac{h(s)-h(s-d)}{d}D(s):=dh(s)−h(s−d)​
(4)

and

Δ=h(x−y′)+h(x′−y)−h(x−y)−h(x′−y′)=d(D(x′−y)−D(x−y)).\Delta =h(x-y')+h(x'-y)-h(x-y)-h(x'-y') =d\bigl(D(x'-y)-D(x-y)\bigr).Δ=h(x−y′)+h(x′−y)−h(x−y)−h(x′−y′)=d(D(x′−y)−D(x−y)).
(5)

Because hhh is strictly convex, DDD is strictly increasing. Since x−y<x′−yx-y<x'-yx−y<x′−y, monotonicity gives D(x−y)<D(x′−y)D(x-y)<D(x'-y)D(x−y)<D(x′−y), hence Δ>0\Delta>0Δ>0.

This property extends by continuity to convex costs that are not strictly convex, such as ∣x−y∣|x-y|∣x−y∣, but then the matching is not necessarily unique. For convex costs, the algorithm to compute an optimal transport is to sort the points: find permutations σX,σY\sigma_X,\sigma_YσX​,σY​ such that

xσX(1)≤xσX(2)≤⋯andyσY(1)≤yσY(2)≤⋯ ,x_{\sigma_X(1)} \leq x_{\sigma_X(2)} \leq \cdots \qquad\text{and}\qquad y_{\sigma_Y(1)} \leq y_{\sigma_Y(2)} \leq \cdots,xσX​(1)​≤xσX​(2)​≤⋯andyσY​(1)​≤yσY​(2)​≤⋯,
(6)

and then map xσX(k)x_{\sigma_X(k)}xσX​(k)​ to yσY(k)y_{\sigma_Y(k)}yσY​(k)​. Equivalently, an optimal transport is σ=σY∘σX−1\sigma=\sigma_Y\circ\sigma_X^{-1}σ=σY​∘σX−1​. The computational cost is therefore O(nlog⁡n)O(n\log n)O(nlogn), using for instance quicksort.

If the distance profile is concave instead of convex, the geometry changes. For costs such as cp(x,y)=∣x−y∣pc_p(x,y)=|x-y|^pcp​(x,y)=∣x−y∣p with 0<p<10<p<10<p<1, splitting a displacement into several smaller displacements is expensive, so optimal matchings tend to create long exchanges rather than the monotone equal-rank pairing. This is the strictly concave regime studied by Gangbo and McCann Gangbo & McCann, 1996.

The real line still gives special structure. After sorting all red and blue points together, the ordered sequence decomposes into maximal alternating chains, and local matching indicators can certify pairs that must be matched in an optimum. Removing such certified pairs and repeating yields an exact hierarchical algorithm for the unit-mass balanced assignment problem, with worst-case complexity O(n2)O(n^2)O(n2) in the framework of Delon, Salomon and Sobolevski Delon et al., 2012. Very concave costs also motivate simpler greedy heuristics, studied for instance by Ottolini and Steinerberger Ottolini & Steinerberger, 2023. The point is that these methods are not generic linear-programming solvers; they use the one-dimensional order and the concavity of the distance profile.

show_book_figure("matching-1d-convex-concave-costs")

One-dimensional assignments for ordered source and target clouds with costs cp(x,y)=∣x−y∣pc_p(x,y)=|x-y|^pcp​(x,y)=∣x−y∣p. The top row uses single-Gaussian source and target clouds; the bottom row uses a denser two-component source and three-component target. For the convex quadratic cost, equal ranks are matched and the segments do not cross. For the concave cost, the optimum creates long crossing exchanges; the ordered line remains useful, but through the alternating-chain structure of concave transport rather than through monotone rearrangement.

Interactive panel. Use the sliders to change the two cost exponents and see how convex costs preserve sorted, non-crossing matches while concave costs favor longer crossing exchanges.

The next figure shows the monotone case more explicitly. The red and blue curves are smooth laws used to generate equal-weight empirical measures; the dots are inverse-CDF samples at common quantile levels. The monotone assignment connects equal ranks.

show_book_figure("matching-1d-quantile-assignment")

One-dimensional optimal matching by quantile sorting. The red and blue curves are smooth laws used to generate equal-weight empirical measures; the dots are inverse-CDF samples at common quantile levels. The monotone assignment connects equal ranks, both for two Gaussian mixtures and for the transport from one central Gaussian toward a three-mode target law.

The interactive panel exposes the point count and the two laws while keeping the monotone equal-rank construction in the background.

n_points = 52
source_law = "two"      # one, two, wide_two, three
target_law = "three"    # one, two, wide_two, three
fig = plot_quantile_matching(
    n=n_points,
    source=source_law,
    target=target_law,
)

Interactive panel. Use the point-count slider and the source/target menus to redraw the one-dimensional monotone assignment. The dots move, but the rule remains equal-rank matching after sorting.

If ϕ:R→R\phi:\RR\to\RRϕ:R→R is increasing, the same technique applies to costs of the form h(∣ϕ(x)−ϕ(y)∣)h(|\phi(x)-\phi(y)|)h(∣ϕ(x)−ϕ(y)∣) after a change of variable. A typical application is grayscale histogram equalization. The empirical cumulative distribution of image luminance values is transported to a prescribed target histogram, for instance a concentrated or reference-image histogram. In one dimension, the monotone rearrangement gives the exact transport map, so the operation is both computationally simple and geometrically faithful: it matches distributions of intensities rather than individual pixels.

show_book_figure("monge-histogram-equalization")

Histogram equalization as one-dimensional Monge transport on pixel intensities. The map is the monotone rearrangement T=Qβ∘FαT=Q_\beta\circ F_\alphaT=Qβ​∘Fα​; here β\betaβ is a truncated Gaussian concentrated near dark intensities. The images are interpolated pointwise by It=(1−t)I+tT(I)I_t=(1-t)I+tT(I)It​=(1−t)I+tT(I), and all histograms share the same vertical scale.

The interactive view below exposes the target mean, target standard deviation, and interpolation time.

target_mean = 0.18
target_sigma = 0.105
interpolation = 0.67  # 0 is the original image, 1 is fully equalized
fig = plot_histogram_equalization(
    target_mean=target_mean,
    target_sigma=target_sigma,
    interpolation=interpolation,
)

Interactive panel. Use the mean, standard-deviation, and time sliders to move the target intensity law and follow the resulting image equalization and histogram deformation.

If hhh is strictly convex, then all optimal assignments are increasing, and if the points are all distinct, this increasing map is unique. If hhh is not strictly convex, for instance c(x,y)=∣x−y∣c(x,y)=|x-y|c(x,y)=∣x−y∣, non-increasing optimal assignments can also exist. The next example shows that this is not a discretization artifact, but a genuine flat direction of the linear cost.

Example: Discrete book-shifting and non-uniqueness for W1\Wass_1W1​

Fix m≥1m\geq1m≥1 and consider two equal-cardinality point clouds

X={1,…,2m},Y={m+1,…,3m},X=\{1,\ldots,2m\}, \qquad Y=\{m+1,\ldots,3m\},X={1,…,2m},Y={m+1,…,3m},
(7)

with uniform weights. The monotone assignment sends iii to i+mi+mi+m and has average cost mmm for c(x,y)=∣x−y∣c(x,y)=|x-y|c(x,y)=∣x−y∣. It is not unique. The discrete book-shifting assignment

Tbook(i)={i+2m,1≤i≤m,i,m<i≤2m,T_{\rm book}(i)= \begin{cases} i+2m, & 1\leq i\leq m,\\ i, & m<i\leq 2m, \end{cases}Tbook​(i)={i+2m,i,​1≤i≤m,m<i≤2m,​
(8)

is also a bijection from XXX to YYY and has the same average cost:

12m∑i=12m∣Tbook(i)−i∣=12m∑i=1m2m=m.\frac1{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{2m}|T_{\rm book}(i)-i| = \frac1{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}2m =m.2m1​i=1∑2m​∣Tbook​(i)−i∣=2m1​i=1∑m​2m=m.
(9)

Optimality follows from the lower bound

12m∑i=12m∣yσ(i)−i∣≥12m∑i=12m(yσ(i)−i)=m,\frac1{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{2m}|y_{\sigma(i)}-i| \geq \frac1{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{2m}(y_{\sigma(i)}-i) =m,2m1​i=1∑2m​∣yσ(i)​−i∣≥2m1​i=1∑2m​(yσ(i)​−i)=m,
(10)

which holds for every assignment because the target sum is fixed. The continuous Monge version of the same book-shifting phenomenon is given later in Example Example: Book-shifting in the Monge problem.

For ∣x−y∣p|x-y|^p∣x−y∣p with p>1p>1p>1, this degeneracy disappears: the monotone assignment has average cost mpm^pmp, whereas the book-shifting assignment has average cost 2p−1mp>mp2^{p-1}m^p>m^p2p−1mp>mp. Strict convexity penalizes concentrating all displacement on half of the points.

Optimal Transport on the Circle¶

The sorting rule on the line has a periodic analogue. Identify the circle with S1=R/Z\mathbb S^1=\RR/\mathbb ZS1=R/Z, let

dS1(x,y):=min⁡k∈Z∣x−y+k∣,cp(x,y):=dS1(x,y)p,p>1.d_{\mathbb S^1}(x,y):=\min_{k\in\mathbb Z}|x-y+k|, \qquad c_p(x,y):=d_{\mathbb S^1}(x,y)^p, \qquad p>1.dS1​(x,y):=k∈Zmin​∣x−y+k∣,cp​(x,y):=dS1​(x,y)p,p>1.
(11)

The only extra datum, compared with the line, is where one opens the circle. Once a cut has been chosen, the circle is unfolded into an interval and the one-dimensional monotone assignment can be used. In the discrete case, changing the cut is the same as applying a cyclic shift to one of the two circular orderings.

Proposition: Discrete Circle Transport by a Cut

Let x1,…,xnx_1,\ldots,x_nx1​,…,xn​ and y1,…,yny_1,\ldots,y_ny1​,…,yn​ be two families of distinct points on S1\mathbb S^1S1, with equal weights. Let x(1),…,x(n)x_{(1)},\ldots,x_{(n)}x(1)​,…,x(n)​ and y(1),…,y(n)y_{(1)},\ldots,y_{(n)}y(1)​,…,y(n)​ denote fixed cyclic orderings, with the convention y(k+n)=y(k)y_{(k+n)}=y_{(k)}y(k+n)​=y(k)​. For the cost cpc_pcp​, p>1p>1p>1, an optimal assignment is one of the cyclic shifts

x(k)⟼y(k+s),k∈{1,…,n},s∈{0,…,n−1},x_{(k)} \longmapsto y_{(k+s)}, \qquad k\in\{1,\ldots,n\}, \qquad s\in\{0,\ldots,n-1\},x(k)​⟼y(k+s)​,k∈{1,…,n},s∈{0,…,n−1},
(12)

and is found by minimizing

∑k=1ndS1 ⁣(x(k),y(k+s))p\sum_{k=1}^n d_{\mathbb S^1}\!\left(x_{(k)},y_{(k+s)}\right)^pk=1∑n​dS1​(x(k)​,y(k+s)​)p
(13)

over the nnn possible shifts. Equivalently, for an optimal shift one can choose a cut θ∈S1∖({xi}i∪{yj}j)\theta\in\mathbb S^1\setminus(\{x_i\}_i\cup\{y_j\}_j)θ∈S1∖({xi​}i​∪{yj​}j​) so that, after lifting all points to (θ,θ+1)(\theta,\theta+1)(θ,θ+1) and sorting them, the optimal matching is the equal-rank monotone matching on this interval.

Proof

Call two matched pairs cyclically inverted if the circular order of their source endpoints is opposite to the circular order of their target endpoints. Among optimal assignments, choose one with the smallest number of such inversions. The elementary exchange step is the circular analogue of the line argument above: if two matched pairs are inverted, then cutting the circle in a gap which does not meet the four endpoints and choosing integer lifts realizes the four geodesic distances involved in the exchange as ordinary distances between two ordered source lifts and two oppositely ordered target lifts. The one-dimensional Monge inequality for the strictly convex function r↦∣r∣pr\mapsto |r|^pr↦∣r∣p then shows that swapping the two targets cannot increase the cost, and decreases it unless the four endpoints are in a degenerate tie configuration.

Thus an optimal assignment can be chosen with no cyclic inversion. A bijection between two finite cyclically ordered sets with no cyclic inversion is a rotation of the order, hence a cyclic shift. This shift specifies how the two cyclic orderings should be opened; after this cut, the rotation becomes an ordinary linear order and the matching is the equal-rank monotone assignment on the unfolded interval. Conversely, each cut gives one such cyclic shift, so minimizing over the finitely many shifts gives an optimal discrete circle assignment. Repeated points or ties are obtained by the same argument after an arbitrarily small perturbation and a limiting passage. This is the discrete form of the fast circle-Monge construction of Delon et al., 2010.

show_book_figure("monge-circle-cut-unfolding")

Optimal transport on the circle by cutting and unfolding. Purple segments show the optimal matching and the green radius marks the chosen cut. The red and blue atoms live on two copies of the circle; the denser point clouds make the cyclic ordering visible. Once the circle is opened at this angle, the same matching appears as a monotone one-dimensional assignment on the interval, with the two green endpoints identified.

Interactive panel. Use the number of points, exponent, and shift controls to open the circle at different cuts and compare the induced cyclic assignments.

show_book_figure("matching-2d-cost-exponent")

Optimal assignments between the same two point clouds for four powers of the Euclidean distance. The source atoms are semi-regular samples in a central disk, while the target atoms are semi-regular samples on a thin annulus; this canonical geometry is reused in later coupling and regularization figures. The feasible set is unchanged, but changing ppp changes the global organization of the permutation: the concave case p=1/2p=1/2p=1/2 penalizes long edges only sublinearly and therefore permits longer exchanges, whereas larger powers increasingly suppress the longest edges.

The interactive panel reuses the same disk-to-annulus geometry and exposes the number of points, the data geometry, and the cost exponents ppp in c(x,y)=∥x−y∥pc(x,y)=\norm{x-y}^pc(x,y)=∥x−y∥p.

n_points_2d = 36
source_shape = "disk"       # disk, annulus, two_blobs, three_blobs, crescent
target_shape = "annulus"    # disk, annulus, two_blobs, three_blobs, crescent
cost_powers = (1, 2, 6)
seed = 2074
fig = plot_cost_power_sweep(
    n_points=n_points_2d,
    source_shape=source_shape,
    target_shape=target_shape,
    cost_powers=cost_powers,
    seed=seed,
)

Interactive panel. Use the exponent sliders to compare how different powers of the distance reshape the same two-dimensional assignment problem.

Rational Weights¶

The strict assignment model is also tied to equal cardinalities and equal weights. As soon as the target resolution changes or the weights are not uniform, a permutation no longer describes the feasible transports. One instead needs a nonnegative transport matrix with prescribed row and column sums; this is the finite-dimensional Kantorovich relaxation developed in the next chapters.

show_book_figure("matching-resolution-and-weights")

From assignments to transport plans, using the same disk-to-annulus geometry. In the balanced equal-weight case, each source atom is matched to one target atom. With a target cloud that has half as many atoms, or with strongly nonuniform target weights, the coupling matrix can merge or split mass; segment thickness and opacity encode its nonzero entries, and blue marker areas encode the prescribed target masses.

The interactive panel below exposes the target resolution, target weights, and regularization level. The first displayed plan is sparse, while positive regularization values show the entropic smoothing used later in the Sinkhorn chapter.

n_source = 36
n_target = 18
weight_mode = "angular"     # uniform, angular, right_heavy
weight_strength = 1.4
epsilons = (0.0, 0.03, 0.12)
fig = plot_regularization_sweep(
    n_source=n_source,
    n_target=n_target,
    source_shape="disk",
    target_shape="annulus",
    cost_power=2,
    epsilons=epsilons,
    weight_mode=weight_mode,
    weight_strength=weight_strength,
    seed=2031,
)

Interactive panel. Use the source and target sizes, weight pattern, and regularization sliders to see how unequal masses and finite resolution change the matching picture.

Proposition: Rational Weights as Duplicated Uniform Matching

Let

μ=∑i=1nkiNδxi,ν=∑j=1mℓjNδyj,∑iki=∑jℓj=N,\mu=\sum_{i=1}^n \frac{k_i}{N}\delta_{x_i}, \qquad \nu=\sum_{j=1}^m \frac{\ell_j}{N}\delta_{y_j}, \qquad \sum_i k_i=\sum_j\ell_j=N,μ=i=1∑n​Nki​​δxi​​,ν=j=1∑m​Nℓj​​δyj​​,i∑​ki​=j∑​ℓj​=N,
(14)

with ki,ℓj∈Nk_i,\ell_j\in\NNki​,ℓj​∈N. The discrete Kantorovich problem between (μ,ν)(\mu,\nu)(μ,ν) is equivalent to the uniform assignment problem obtained by replacing each xix_ixi​ by kik_iki​ identical copies and each yjy_jyj​ by ℓj\ell_jℓj​ identical copies. More precisely, after multiplying transport masses by NNN, optimal couplings correspond to optimal integer count matrices (nij)(n_{ij})(nij​) with row sums kik_iki​ and column sums ℓj\ell_jℓj​, and these count matrices are exactly the collapsed form of assignments between the duplicated clouds.

Proof

Any assignment between the duplicated source and target clouds defines integers nijn_{ij}nij​ counting how many copied particles of type xix_ixi​ are matched to copied particles of type yjy_jyj​. These counts satisfy ∑jnij=ki\sum_j n_{ij}=k_i∑j​nij​=ki​ and ∑inij=ℓj\sum_i n_{ij}=\ell_j∑i​nij​=ℓj​, and the associated coupling Pij=nij/NP_{ij}=n_{ij}/NPij​=nij​/N has marginals ki/Nk_i/Nki​/N and ℓj/N\ell_j/Nℓj​/N. The assignment cost is

1N∑i,jnijc(xi,yj)=∑i,jPijc(xi,yj).\frac1N\sum_{i,j} n_{ij}c(x_i,y_j) = \sum_{i,j}P_{ij}c(x_i,y_j).N1​i,j∑​nij​c(xi​,yj​)=i,j∑​Pij​c(xi​,yj​).
(15)

Conversely, any nonnegative integer count matrix with those row and column sums can be realized by matching the corresponding duplicated particles. Finally, the transportation constraint matrix is totally unimodular, so the linear problem with integer supplies and demands has an optimal integer count matrix. Thus the optimum of the rational-weight Kantorovich problem is the same as the optimum of the duplicated uniform assignment problem.

show_book_figure("matching-rational-duplication")

Rational weights as duplicated uniform matchings, using the same disk-to-annulus geometry with fewer displayed atoms. The red and blue locations are kept fixed, while disk areas encode the integer multiplicities kik_iki​ and ℓj\ell_jℓj​. Solving the assignment problem after duplicating particles produces several collapsed segments attached to high-multiplicity atoms; this is the integer count matrix of the proposition.

Interactive panel. Use the site and multiplicity sliders to see how rational weights can be represented by duplicated unit masses before solving an ordinary matching problem.

Two-Dimensional Assignments¶

This efficient strategy to compute the OT in one dimension does not extend to higher dimensions. In two dimensions, as already noted by Monge, optimal segments for the Euclidean distance cannot cross.

Proposition: Non-Crossing Optimal Matchings

In dimension two, for c(x,y)=∥x−y∥c(x,y)=\norm{x-y}c(x,y)=∥x−y∥, if σ\sigmaσ is an optimal assignment, then the segments [xi,yσ(i)][x_i,y_{\sigma(i)}][xi​,yσ(i)​] cannot cross.

Proof

If two segments [xi,yσ(i)][x_i,y_{\sigma(i)}][xi​,yσ(i)​] and [xj,yσ(j)][x_j,y_{\sigma(j)}][xj​,yσ(j)​] cross at an interior point zzz, then the triangle inequality gives

∥xi−yσ(j)∥+∥xj−yσ(i)∥<∥xi−yσ(i)∥+∥xj−yσ(j)∥.\norm{x_i-y_{\sigma(j)}}+\norm{x_j-y_{\sigma(i)}} < \norm{x_i-y_{\sigma(i)}}+\norm{x_j-y_{\sigma(j)}}.∥∥​xi​−yσ(j)​∥∥​+∥∥​xj​−yσ(i)​∥∥​<∥∥​xi​−yσ(i)​∥∥​+∥∥​xj​−yσ(j)​∥∥​.
(16)

The assignment obtained by swapping (σ(i),σ(j))(\sigma(i),\sigma(j))(σ(i),σ(j)) therefore has a strictly smaller cost, contradicting optimality.

This property alone is not enough to lead to an efficient algorithm. Non-crossing is only a necessary local test, not a compact certificate of optimality. For instance, if nnn sources and nnn targets are placed alternately on the boundary of a convex polygon, the number of non-crossing perfect matchings is the Catalan number

Cn=1n+1(2nn)∼4nπn3/2.C_n=\frac{1}{n+1}\binom{2n}{n} \sim \frac{4^n}{\sqrt{\pi}n^{3/2}}.Cn​=n+11​(n2n​)∼π​n3/24n​.
(17)
Remark: Catalan count of alternating non-crossing matchings

The count follows from the standard Catalan recurrence. Fix one red vertex rrr. In a non-crossing perfect matching, if rrr is matched to a blue vertex bbb, the chord [r,b][r,b][r,b] splits the polygon into two smaller polygons. Since the boundary colors alternate, each side contains the same number of red and blue vertices. If one side contains kkk red and kkk blue vertices, the other contains n−1−kn-1-kn−1−k red and n−1−kn-1-kn−1−k blue vertices. Non-crossing matchings on the two sides are independent, because no segment can cross the chord [r,b][r,b][r,b]. Thus, denoting by MnM_nMn​ the number of such matchings, one has

M0=1,Mn=∑k=0n−1MkMn−1−k.M_0=1, \qquad M_n=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} M_k M_{n-1-k}.M0​=1,Mn​=k=0∑n−1​Mk​Mn−1−k​.
(18)

This recurrence characterizes the Catalan numbers, hence Mn=CnM_n=C_nMn​=Cn​.

Thus even after forbidding crossings, an exhaustive search remains exponential. The two-segment swap in the proof above is nevertheless useful: it explains why a crossing matching cannot be optimal, but it does not select among the exponentially many planar matchings that survive this local test.

Algorithm: One-dimensional sorting assignment

Input: Equal-weight point clouds (xi)i=1n(x_i)_{i=1}^n(xi​)i=1n​, (yj)j=1n(y_j)_{j=1}^n(yj​)j=1n​ on R\RRR; convex cost h(x−y)h(x-y)h(x−y).

Output: Optimal permutation σ\sigmaσ.

Sort source and target points: xσX(1)≤⋯≤xσX(n),yσY(1)≤⋯≤yσY(n).x_{\sigma_X(1)}\leq\cdots\leq x_{\sigma_X(n)}, \qquad y_{\sigma_Y(1)}\leq\cdots\leq y_{\sigma_Y(n)}.xσX​(1)​≤⋯≤xσX​(n)​,yσY​(1)​≤⋯≤yσY​(n)​.

For k=1,…,nk=1,\ldots,nk=1,…,n do:

Match xσX(k)x_{\sigma_X(k)}xσX​(k)​ with yσY(k)y_{\sigma_Y(k)}yσY​(k)​.

Return σ=σY∘σX−1.\sigma=\sigma_Y\circ\sigma_X^{-1}.σ=σY​∘σX−1​.

Algorithm: Concave line matching by local indicators

Input: Unit-mass source and target points on R\RRR; strictly concave distance cost.

Output: Optimal concave-cost matching MMM.

Sort combined red-blue sequence on the line.

Decompose it into maximal alternating chains.

Initialize: Set M=∅M=\emptysetM=∅.

While unmatched points remain do:

For each active alternating chain do:

Compute local matching indicators Delon et al., 2012.

If an indicator certifies a pair (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) then:

Update M←M∪{(i,j)}M\leftarrow M\cup\{(i,j)\}M←M∪{(i,j)}.

Remove points iii and jjj.

Recompute only chains affected by removals.

Return MMM.

Algorithm: Circle assignment by cutting

Input: Equal-weight points (xi)i=1n(x_i)_{i=1}^n(xi​)i=1n​, (yj)j=1n(y_j)_{j=1}^n(yj​)j=1n​ on S1\mathbb S^1S1; cost dS1pd_{\mathbb S^1}^pdS1p​.

Output: Optimal cyclic assignment.

Let x(1),…,x(n)x_{(1)},\ldots,x_{(n)}x(1)​,…,x(n)​ and y(1),…,y(n)y_{(1)},\ldots,y_{(n)}y(1)​,…,y(n)​ be the points sorted by increasing angle from a fixed origin.

For s=0,…,n−1s=0,\ldots,n-1s=0,…,n−1 do:

Es=∑k=1ndS1 ⁣(x(k),y(k+s))p,y(k+n)=y(k).E_s=\sum_{k=1}^n d_{\mathbb S^1}\!\left(x_{(k)},y_{(k+s)}\right)^p, \qquad y_{(k+n)}=y_{(k)}.Es​=∑k=1n​dS1​(x(k)​,y(k+s)​)p,y(k+n)​=y(k)​.

Set s⋆=min⁡arg min⁡0≤s<nEss^\star=\min\argmin_{0\leq s<n}E_ss⋆=minargmin0≤s<n​Es​.

Set θcut\theta_{\rm cut}θcut​ in an empty arc separating two consecutive matched pairs for the shift s⋆s^\stars⋆.

Replace every angle by its representative in [θcut,θcut+2π)[\theta_{\rm cut},\theta_{\rm cut}+2\pi)[θcut​,θcut​+2π). Return x(k)↦y(k+s⋆)x_{(k)}\mapsto y_{(k+s^\star)}x(k)​↦y(k+s⋆)​.

Matching Algorithms¶

This section briefly locates matching within classical combinatorial optimization. Its main point is that efficient algorithms exist, but their cleanest analysis is obtained only after introducing the linear-programming viewpoint.

Efficient algorithms exist to solve the optimal matching problem. The most well-known are the Hungarian method and auction algorithms Kuhn, 1955Bertsekas, 1981Bertsekas, 1992. Auction algorithms use prices on the target points: each source bids for the target with largest reduced profit, the target price is increased, and the process terminates when the ϵ\epsilonϵ-complementary slackness conditions are satisfied. For integer costs, choosing ϵ<1/n\epsilon<1/nϵ<1/n gives an exact optimum after a finite number of bids Bertsekas, 1992. The dual chapter revisits this algorithm after Kantorovich duality and explains why it is a dual price method, parallel in spirit to Sinkhorn scaling.

Hungarian Primal-Dual Method¶

The Hungarian method is best understood as a certificate-building algorithm for the assignment linear program. It maintains a partial matching MMM and dual prices (ui,vj)(u_i,v_j)(ui​,vj​) satisfying

ui+vj≤Ci,j∀i,j.u_i+v_j\leq C_{i,j} \qquad \forall i,j.ui​+vj​≤Ci,j​∀i,j.
(19)

The equality graph E(u,v)={(i,j):ui+vj=Ci,j}E(u,v)=\{(i,j):u_i+v_j=C_{i,j}\}E(u,v)={(i,j):ui​+vj​=Ci,j​} contains the edges whose reduced cost is zero. The algorithm only augments MMM along alternating paths made of equality edges. Starting from an unmatched source, it grows an alternating tree with source set SSS and target set TTT. If the tree reaches an unmatched target, the matching is augmented along the path. If no such edge exists, the dual variables are shifted by the smallest slack

δ=min⁡i∈S, j∉T(Ci,j−ui−vj),ui←ui+δ (i∈S),vj←vj−δ (j∈T).\delta=\min_{i\in S,\ j\notin T}\bigl(C_{i,j}-u_i-v_j\bigr), \qquad u_i\leftarrow u_i+\delta\ (i\in S), \qquad v_j\leftarrow v_j-\delta\ (j\in T).δ=i∈S, j∈/Tmin​(Ci,j​−ui​−vj​),ui​←ui​+δ (i∈S),vj​←vj​−δ (j∈T).
(20)

This update preserves all inequalities ui+vj≤Ci,ju_i+v_j\leq C_{i,j}ui​+vj​≤Ci,j​, keeps the current alternating tree tight, and creates at least one new equality edge leaving SSS. Maintaining these slacks incrementally gives the standard O(n3)O(n^3)O(n3) implementation for an n×nn\times nn×n assignment problem.

The following figure summarizes actual iterates by displaying only the evolving partial assignment: unmatched rows are shown as flat rows to keep a fixed matrix format, and matched rows are shown as one-hot rows.

show_book_figure("matching-hungarian-progression")

Matrix view of actual Hungarian primal-dual iterates on a diagonally dominant ordered one-dimensional squared-distance assignment. Each panel records the current partial assignment state: unassigned rows are kept flat, while assigned rows are one-hot. The snapshots are taken at initialization and after two, four, six and eight augmentations; for this pedagogical instance the partial assignments grow along the diagonal, and the final matrix is the identity assignment certified by complementary slackness.

Interactive panel. Use the size, jitter, and seed controls to regenerate the assignment instance and inspect snapshots of the Hungarian augmentation process.

Proposition: Correctness and Complexity of the Hungarian Primal-Dual Method

Assume the Hungarian method terminates with a perfect matching σ\sigmaσ contained in the equality graph

E(u,v)={(i,j):ui+vj=Ci,j},E(u,v)=\{(i,j):u_i+v_j=C_{i,j}\},E(u,v)={(i,j):ui​+vj​=Ci,j​},
(21)

where (u,v)(u,v)(u,v) is dual feasible, i.e. ui+vj≤Ci,ju_i+v_j\leq C_{i,j}ui​+vj​≤Ci,j​ for all (i,j)(i,j)(i,j). Then σ\sigmaσ is an optimal assignment. Moreover, the usual Hungarian updates terminate after finitely many augmentations. With maintained slacks, the method uses O(n3)O(n^3)O(n3) arithmetic operations.

Proof

For any permutation τ\tauτ, dual feasibility gives

∑iCi,τ(i)≥∑i(ui+vτ(i))=∑iui+∑jvj.\sum_i C_{i,\tau(i)} \geq \sum_i (u_i+v_{\tau(i)}) = \sum_i u_i+\sum_j v_j.i∑​Ci,τ(i)​≥i∑​(ui​+vτ(i)​)=i∑​ui​+j∑​vj​.
(22)

This is the weak duality lower bound. If σ\sigmaσ is contained in the equality graph, then

∑iCi,σ(i)=∑iui+∑jvj,\sum_i C_{i,\sigma(i)} = \sum_i u_i+\sum_j v_j,i∑​Ci,σ(i)​=i∑​ui​+j∑​vj​,
(23)

so the primal cost of σ\sigmaσ reaches the dual lower bound and is optimal.

It remains to justify finite termination and the complexity bound. At each successful augmentation, the matching cardinality increases by one, so there are at most nnn augmentations. During one augmentation phase, the algorithm grows an alternating tree in the equality graph. If no augmenting path is available, the dual update uses the smallest slack of an edge leaving the current tree. For edges inside the tree, adding δ\deltaδ to source labels and subtracting δ\deltaδ from target labels preserves tightness; for edges from SSS to TcT^cTc, the definition of δ\deltaδ preserves feasibility and makes at least one new edge tight; all other inequalities are unchanged or become looser. Thus the reachable sets strictly grow between two failed augmentation attempts, and they can grow at most nnn times within one phase. If the current slacks min⁡i∈S(Ci,j−ui−vj)\min_{i\in S}(C_{i,j}-u_i-v_j)mini∈S​(Ci,j​−ui​−vj​) are updated when a source enters SSS, each tree expansion costs O(n)O(n)O(n). A phase therefore costs O(n2)O(n^2)O(n2), and the nnn phases give O(n3)O(n^3)O(n3) operations. Hence the method reaches a perfect optimal matching.

Algorithm: Hungarian primal-dual augmentation

Input: Square cost matrix C∈Rn×nC\in\RR^{n\times n}C∈Rn×n.

Output: Minimum-cost perfect matching MMM.

Initialize: Set ui=min⁡jCiju_i=\min_j C_{ij}ui​=minj​Cij​ and vj=0v_j=0vj​=0.

Set M=∅M=\emptysetM=∅.

While MMM is not perfect do:

Build equality graph: E(u,v)={(i,j):ui+vj=Ci,j}.E(u,v)=\{(i,j):u_i+v_j=C_{i,j}\}.E(u,v)={(i,j):ui​+vj​=Ci,j​}.

Set root i0=min⁡{i: i is unmatched in M}i_0=\min\{i:\ i\text{ is unmatched in }M\}i0​=min{i: i is unmatched in M}.

Set reached sets S={i0}S=\{i_0\}S={i0​} and T=∅T=\emptysetT=∅; clear parent pointers.

While TTT contains no unmatched target do:

If NE(S)∖T=∅N_E(S)\setminus T=\emptysetNE​(S)∖T=∅ then:

Compute δ=min⁡i∈S, j∉T(Ci,j−ui−vj)\delta=\min_{i\in S,\ j\notin T}\bigl(C_{i,j}-u_i-v_j\bigr)δ=mini∈S, j∈/T​(Ci,j​−ui​−vj​).

Update ui←ui+δu_i\leftarrow u_i+\deltaui​←ui​+δ for i∈Si\in Si∈S and vj←vj−δv_j\leftarrow v_j-\deltavj​←vj​−δ for j∈Tj\in Tj∈T.

Refresh equality graph E(u,v)E(u,v)E(u,v).

Set J=NE(S)∖TJ=N_E(S)\setminus TJ=NE​(S)∖T.

For each j∈Jj\in Jj∈J in increasing order do:

Add jjj to TTT and set parent row p(j)=min⁡{i∈S:(i,j)∈E(u,v)}p(j)=\min\{i\in S:(i,j)\in E(u,v)\}p(j)=min{i∈S:(i,j)∈E(u,v)}.

If jjj is matched to i′i'i′ in MMM then set S←S∪{i′}S\leftarrow S\cup\{i'\}S←S∪{i′} and q(i′)=jq(i')=jq(i′)=j.

Set j0=min⁡{j∈T: j is unmatched in M}j_0=\min\{j\in T:\ j\text{ is unmatched in }M\}j0​=min{j∈T: j is unmatched in M}.

Set j=j0j=j_0j=j0​.

While jjj is defined do:

Set i=p(j)i=p(j)i=p(j).

Set M←M∪{(i,j)}M\leftarrow M\cup\{(i,j)\}M←M∪{(i,j)}.

Set jold=q(i)j_{\rm old}=q(i)jold​=q(i).

If joldj_{\rm old}jold​ is defined then set M←M∖{(i,jold)}M\leftarrow M\setminus\{(i,j_{\rm old})\}M←M∖{(i,jold​)}. Set j=joldj=j_{\rm old}j=jold​.

Return MMM.

References¶
  1. Kuhn, H. W. (1955). The Hungarian Method for the Assignment Problem. Naval Research Logistics Quarterly, 2(1–2), 83–97. 10.1002/nav.3800020109
  2. Bertsekas, D. P. (1992). Auction algorithms for network flow problems: a tutorial introduction. Computational Optimization and Applications, 1(1), 7–66.
  3. Gangbo, W., & McCann, R. J. (1996). The geometry of optimal transportation. Acta Mathematica, 177(2), 113–161.
  4. Delon, J., Salomon, J., & Sobolevski, A. (2012). Local matching indicators for transport problems with concave costs. SIAM Journal on Discrete Mathematics, 26(2), 801–827.
  5. Ottolini, A., & Steinerberger, S. (2023). Greedy Matching in Optimal Transport with Concave Cost. arXiv Preprint arXiv:2307.03140.
  6. Delon, J., Salomon, J., & Sobolevski, A. (2010). Fast transport optimization for Monge costs on the circle. SIAM Journal on Applied Mathematics, 70(7), 2239–2258.
  7. Bertsekas, D. P. (1981). A new algorithm for the assignment problem. Mathematical Programming, 21(1), 152–171.
